perturbation.original_text: Subject to the provisions of Clauses 4 and 7 hereof, the Receiving Party undertakes: (a) to keep confidential and not disclose to any third party, except with the written permission of the Disclosing Party, any part, or the whole, of any Proprietary Information disclosed to it under this Agreem ent;
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the provisions of Clauses 4 and 7 hereof, the Receiving Party undertakes: (a) to endeavor to keep confidential and not disclose to any third party, except when it feels it is reasonably necessary to do so, any part, or the whole, of any Proprietary Information disclosed to it under this Agreem ent;
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation to keep information confidential unless explicitly permitted by the disclosing party. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'to keep confidential and not disclose' with 'to endeavor to keep confidential' and adding 'except when it feels it is reasonably necessary to do so.' This makes the obligation weaker and introduces a subjective standard for disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain trade secret confidentiality. Under the Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018, the misappropriation of trade secrets is unlawful. The 'endeavor to keep confidential' and 'reasonably necessary' clauses introduce subjective loopholes that would make it more difficult to prove misappropriation, as the Receiving Party can argue they 'endeavored' to keep it secret and felt disclosure was 'reasonably necessary,' undermining the protection offered by the regulation. The original text provided stronger legal grounds for enforcement, which this weakens.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. The original text had a clearer obligation, while the changed text uses 'endeavor to keep confidential' and 'reasonably necessary', introducing subjective loopholes. This makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation, as the Receiving Party can argue they 'endeavored' to keep it secret and felt disclosure was 'reasonably necessary'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions "The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018", suggesting that the change could undermine trade secret protection, which the Law Explanation confirms. The modification introduces ambiguity, making the agreement difficult to enforce, which falls under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction. Therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in force for a period of two (2) years following the date first above written after which it will terminate unless renewed by mutual consent in writing or to the extent that it is superseded by another agreement or contract between the Parties. The obli gations and restrictions relating to the disclosure and use of Proprietary Information shall however survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of seven (7) years.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in force for approximately two (2) years following the date first above written, subject to possible early termination if circumstances warrant. The obligations and restrictions relating to the disclosure and use of Proprietary Information may survive the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a clear two-year term with a seven-year survival clause for confidentiality. The modified text makes the term 'approximately two years' and adds 'subject to possible early termination if circumstances warrant,' introducing ambiguity. The survival clause becomes 'may survive,' further weakening the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Limitation Act 1980 establishes statutory time limits for bringing legal claims. For contract claims, this is typically six years from the date the cause of action accrued. By making the confidentiality obligations potentially non-binding post-termination ('may survive'), the modified text creates uncertainty as to the duration of these obligations, potentially conflicting with the established six-year limitation period for contract claims related to breaches of confidentiality after the initial term. The original text clearly defined the survival period as 7 years.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the duration of the agreement and the survival of confidentiality obligations. The original text provided a fixed two-year term and a seven-year survival period for confidentiality. The changed text uses 'approximately two years' and 'may survive,' weakening these obligations. The Law Explanation states that this creates uncertainty regarding the duration of these obligations, which could conflict with statutory time limits for bringing legal claims. While the scraped snippets do not directly address specific time limits, the Law Explanation's reasoning about creating uncertainty supports the existence of a contradiction. The original text clearly defined the survival period as 7 years, while the changed text weakens this obligation.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall attempt to solve any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement by means of alternative dispute resolution such as b ut without limitation mediati on or mini -trial. Upon the occurrence of the dispute, the Parties shall define the type and the rules for the implementation of such alternative resolution. The Parties expressly agree that after a period of two (2) months after the occurrence of the dis pute and provided that the dispute remains unsolved, it shall be finally settled under the rules of Arbitration of the London Court of International Arbitration by one or more arbitrators appointed in accordance with the said rules.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties should consider attempting to solve any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement by means of alternative dispute resolution. The Parties may define the type and the rules for the implementation of such alternative resolution. If the dispute remains unsolved, it may be settled under the rules of Arbitration of the London Court of International Arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates an attempt at alternative dispute resolution (ADR) before arbitration. The modified text changes 'shall attempt' to 'should consider,' 'shall define' to 'may define,' and 'it shall be finally settled' to 'it may be settled,' making ADR optional and arbitration not guaranteed, introducing significant ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Arbitration Act 1996, Section 9, provides for a stay of legal proceedings where a valid arbitration agreement exists. By weakening the obligation to arbitrate ('may be settled') and making ADR optional, the modified text undermines the enforceability of arbitration and contradicts the intention of the Arbitration Act 1996, which aims to support and facilitate arbitration. The original text made arbitration a mandatory step after failed ADR.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original contract's commitment to both ADR and arbitration. The original contract mandates an attempt at ADR ('shall attempt') and guarantees arbitration if ADR fails ('it shall be finally settled'). The modified text changes these obligations to discretionary options ('should consider,' 'may define,' 'it may be settled'). This change undermines the enforceability of arbitration, as the obligation to arbitrate is no longer guaranteed. The Law Explanation states this change contradicts the intention of the Arbitration Act 1996, which aims to support and facilitate arbitration. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the Arbitration Act 1996, supports the relevance of this law. Therefore, by introducing ambiguity and weakening the commitment to arbitration, the modified text creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Proprietary Information disclosed hereunder, and any copies thereof made by a Receiving Party, shall be and remain the Disclosing Party's property and shall be delivered up promptly by the Receiving Party to the Disclosing Party on receipt of the Disclosing Party's written request therefor.
perturbation.changed_text: All Proprietary Information disclosed hereunder, and any copies thereof made by a Receiving Party, are generally considered the Disclosing Party's property and should, where feasible, be returned by the Receiving Party to the Disclosing Party upon request.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation for the Receiving Party to return all proprietary information and copies promptly upon written request. The modified text weakens this obligation significantly by changing 'shall be and remain the Disclosing Party's property' to 'are generally considered the Disclosing Party's property' and 'shall be delivered up promptly' to 'should, where feasible, be returned.' This introduces ambiguity and provides loopholes for non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under English law, conversion is a tort involving interference with someone's property rights. The Torts (Interference with Goods) Act 1977 addresses this. The original clause clearly establishes that the information remains the Disclosing Party's property, and the Receiving Party has an obligation to return it. By weakening this obligation ('generally considered' and 'where feasible'), the modified text makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to assert their property rights and potentially renders the Receiving Party's failure to return the information less likely to be considered conversion, contradicting the protections afforded by tort law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Torts (Interference with Goods) Act 1977 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia United Kingdom legislation Torts (Interference with Goods) Act 1977 Act of Parliament Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to amend the law concerning conversion and other torts affecting goods. Citation 1977 c. 32 Dates Royal assent 22 July 1977 Other legislation Repeals/revokes Disposal of Uncollected Goods Act 1952 Status: Current legislation Text of statute as originally enacted Text of the Torts (Interference with Goods) Act 1977 as in force today (including any amendments) within the United Kingdom, from legislation.gov.uk . The Torts (Interference with Goods) Act 1977 (c. 32) is an act of Parliament to amend the law in England, Wales and Northern Ireland [ 1 ] concerning conversion and other torts affecting goods. [ 2 ] The passage of the law was prompted by the 18th Report of the Law Reform Committee , [ 3 ] although the scope of the legislation is considerably reduced from the recommendations of the report. [ 1 ] The act abolishes detinue [ 4 ] and attempts to simplify the remaining actions in tort. [ 5 ] See also [ edit ] Trespass in English law References [ edit ] ^ a b Thornely, J. W. A. (1977). "New Torts for Old or Old Torts Refurbished?" . The Cambridge Law Journal . 36 (2): 248– 251. ISSN 0008-1973 . ^ "Torts (Interference with Goods) Act 1977" . legislation.gov.uk . Retrieved 4 December 2015 . ^ Bentley, D. J. (1972). "A New-Found Haliday: The Eighteenth Report of the Law Reform Committee (Conversion and Detinue)" . The Modern Law Review . 35 (2): 171– 175. ISSN 0026-7961 . ^ Steele, Jenny (2014). Tort Law (3 ed.). Oxford University Press. p. 904. ISBN 9780199671403 . ^ Sacks, Vera (1978). "Torts (Interference with Goods) Act 1977" . The Modern Law Review . 41 (6): 713– 721. ISSN 0026-7961 . perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity that weakens the original obligation to return proprietary information, potentially contradicting property rights protections. The original text's 'shall be delivered up promptly' is replaced by 'should, where feasible, be returned,' which provides loopholes for non-compliance. The Law Explanation suggests this could make it harder for the Disclosing Party to assert property rights, potentially affecting the Receiving Party's liability under tort law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that the Torts (Interference with Goods) Act 1977 amends the law concerning conversion and other torts affecting goods, which indicates a legal framework for protecting property rights against interference. The change weakens the original clear property rights obligation and return requirement.
